messages.content:
Summarize the following text in five sentences or less.:
AMD vs. Intel - Which CPU is best suited to AI and deep learning?
Desktop processors - AMD vs Intel: Which is better for AI and deep learning?
The world of tech has always run rife with direct competition. Whether it’s Apple vs. Microsoft, Betamax vs. VHS, Nintendo vs. Sega or Google vs. Yahoo, wherever there is innovation there are always heads butting. Indeed, it could be argued that it’s this competition that catalyses innovation and cultivates real lasting progress.

In the case of processors, the duelling sector leaders have always been Intel and AMD and it’s a rivalry with some serious history; a history that’s still being written. But whilst there has been plenty of discussion on the benefits and drawbacks of each manufacturer’s processors and chipsets in terms of gaming performance, there has been precious little discussion on how they both fare in artificial intelligence (AI) and deep learning (DL) applications.

Of course, there are a wealth of differences and similarities to pick through, some abundantly obvious and some a little less so. To gain some wider perspective, we’ll be focusing on the fundamentals in an effort to provide a balanced argument and a helpful guide for those still on the fence about which team to throw down their chips on.

​​​

AMD vs. Intel - Which CPU is best suited to AI and deep learning?​

Click and drag to move
​

AMD vs Intel: Price
Saving costs is always a major concern, particularly for R&D departments that might be putting together multiple rigs on a svelte budget. That’s why AMD has become so popular with everyone, from gamers to researchers, in recent years. 

Traditionally, AMD has always been seen as the underdog in this story and, as is often the case with underdogs, their products have generally been priced more affordably. This has led to some corners of the market to believe that Intel chips are better simply because they’re more expensive, which is certainly not the case. At least, not anymore.

Whilst AMD processors might have initially been priced to match their lower performance when compared to Intel, in recent years, AMD has caught up with (and some might argue overtaken) their closest competitor in the value-for-money stakes as far as performance is concerned.

Currently, however, when examining the price and performance of high-end CPUs, AMD Threadripper-based chips offer the greatest bang for your buck, at least in terms of sheer power.

AMD vs Intel: Performance
Deep learning workstations require bandwidth, and lots of it, so one of the primary concerns when choosing your CPU is the number of PCIe lanes that are on offer. The PCIe lanes on your CPU are primarily assigned to your graphics card (GPU), and each of your graphics cards will require 16 PCIe lanes otherwise known as 16x, to run at 'full-speed'. 

GPUs still do the majority of the heavy lifting within AI/DL workloads, so Deep learning workstations generally require a CPU with at least 40 PCIe lanes, as this will give more than enough bandwidth to run at least 2 GPUs simultaneously.

'Consumer-grade' CPUs, such as Intel's core range, or AMD's Ryzen chips will only offer you 16 PCIe lanes, so you really need to look at Intel's XEON lineup, which offers 40-64 lanes or if 64 lanes isn’t enough for you, then AMD's Threadripper or EPYC range, which provide up to 88 and 128 PCIe 4.0 lanes respectively (double the bandwidth of PCIe 3.0).

AMD vs Intel: Specs
The number of cores in the processor is an incredibly important consideration, particularly given the fact that AI and DL are incredibly complicated and power-hungry workloads that require enough cores to pre-process datasets and run multiple applications and containers simultaneously.

AMD’s Threadripper packs up to a whopping 64 cores and 128 threads, which is beyond anything offered by Intel outside of the server space - it’s obvious why so many serious R&D divisions are turning to AMD over the more established ‘tentpole’ brand. 

AMD’s prevalence in this sector has just been confirmed by NVIDIA, who recently chose AMD (its own major rival in the gaming performance sector) over Intel to provide the processors for its new DGX A100 deep learning system; specifically its EPYC server processors. Intel’s competitor (the Xeon) includes a much-heralded DL Boost feature, which drastically speeds up DL interferencing operations. But in terms of sheer power, AMD’s EPYC has them beat hands down.

Memory channels and disk I/O bandwidth are also considerations here – particularly the former, as RAM remains the cheapest way of maximising your processor's potential. 

AMD vs Intel: Recent releases
Of course, the relatively recent introduction of Intel’s new “Cooper Lake” 3rd Gen Intel Xeon Scalable processors has certainly set the cat amongst the pigeons. These Intel CPUs have been designed from the ground up for AI and analytics workloads running in data centres, network and intelligent-edge environments. 

The company boasts that these are the first chips with built-in ‘bfloat16’ support, which is a compact numeric format that uses half the bits of today’s FP32 format. In theory, that means it should be twice as fast as there is half as much to process. 

It’s also worth noting that last year, Apple ceased utilising Intel processors in its computers and instead started using its own ARM-based chips; the same core designs used by its iPhones and iPads. It would appear this was a move made to consolidate the Apple portfolio, with all systems running on the same basic architecture.  

From an AI/DL perspective, this is significant, as Apple’s A12 “Bionic” chip includes a so-called “Neural Engine,” which is specifically dedicated to machine learning tasks. Indeed, there are corners of the market that herald the new M1 Apple machines as quite substantial machine learning rigs for the money. Still, we imagine most researchers would prefer to stick with the PC-based devils they know, at least for the foreseeable future.

In fact, recent news that Intel will be working with Israeli deep learning specialists Deci has further emboldened the company’s stake in the AI game. This new collaboration seeks to enable deep learning inference at scale on Intel CPUs, reducing costs and latency, and enabling new applications of deep learning inference.

The idea is that new deep learning tasks will be able to be performed in a real-time environment on edge devices and companies that use large scale inference scenarios can dramatically cut cloud or datacenter cost, simply by changing the inference hardware from GPU to Intel CPU.

AMD vs Intel: Future Updates
The future is all to play for. AMD might have had a very successful few years recently while Intel has enjoyed slightly less critical praise. However, Intel has learned from their mistakes and are looking set to strike back hard but even their own CFO has opened up about the fact they are lagging behind AMD in terms of sheer power. 

AMD appears to have chosen their lane and that lane is one of pure power - more cores and more threads than the competitor at any cost. Intel, meanwhile, seems to be quite content to focus more on the entry level, laptop processor and integrated graphics markets, which might not have much to offer for the AI and DL sector.

AMD vs Intel: Core processors or clock speed?
More cores and more power are always better, right? You might think so, and well, you'd be mostly right, as pre-processing, on the whole, is a massively multi-threaded process. And of course, you need to ensure that the CPU has enough threads to feed the GPUs to prevent any bottlenecks.

Although, there are still certain cases where single-core speed counts are especially important. When calculations must be completed sequentially, for example, a CPU with less cores and a higher frequency is capable of out-performing a CPU with many slower cores.
 
Logic dictates that, Intel is your best bet for a higher frequency clock speed, and you should go with AMD for a higher core/thread count. But there are so many other factors to consider that it would be foolish to reduce the choice to such a binary concept.

One area in which Intel has AMD beat on AI/DL is on the software front. The proprietary deep learning software optimisation created by Intel that only runs on Intel CPUs is reason enough for some to avoid AMD altogether and then there’s the Intel Optane memory factor, which allows Intel CPUs to use Optane memory, which can hit higher performance than even the fastest DRAM SSDs. It’s also worth noting that motherboards built for gaming are generally easier to overclock, and this much is true whether you opt for AMD or Intel.

Playing with Power
From a performance perspective, the consensus is that, whilst Intel processors generally contain fewer cores than their AMD counterparts, they provide a slightly stronger single-core performance and for many, that might be enough to swing it. With AMD, however, you’re getting more cores for your money and with many deep learning and AI frameworks requiring a heavier workload from our machines, sometimes raw power is really what’s needed. 

So, would we say, in the battle of Intel vs. AMD, that the little guy has won for once? Not necessarily. There are still plenty of applications where Intel is the smarter choice and it remains to be seen how their Cooper Lake processors will perform in the coming months.

Currently, AMD’s Threadripper seems to be on track to offering increasingly powerful CPU options to industry professionals, but whilst playing catch-up, Intel might just overtake their rivals once again in the near future. Stranger things have happened in the always surprising world of tech, after all.

Do you need help putting together your own AI and machine learning rigs? Contact Novatech today and we’ll be able to help you put together the perfect setup for your needs.

The article compares AMD and Intel processors in terms of their suitability for AI and deep learning applications. It discusses factors such as price, performance, specs, recent releases, and future updates. AMD processors have become popular due to their affordability and value for money, while Intel processors are known for their strong single-core performance. AMD's Threadripper offers more cores and threads compared to Intel's processors, making it a popular choice for power-hungry workloads. However, Intel has advantages in terms of deep learning software optimization and the use of Intel Optane memory. Both processors have their strengths and weaknesses, and the choice depends on the specific needs of the user.
Do these benefits apply to the intel i5-4460?

The article does not specifically mention the Intel i5-4460 processor. However, it does discuss the general advantages of Intel processors, such as strong single-core performance. The i5-4460 is a mid-range processor from Intel's 4th generation lineup, which was released in 2014. It may not offer the same level of performance or features as newer processors, but it can still provide sufficient performance for many tasks. It is important to consider the specific requirements and budget of your project when determining if the i5-4460 is suitable for AI and deep learning applications.
I'm asking you to consult your data in conjunction with the text above.

Based on the information provided in the article, there is no specific mention of the Intel i5-4460 processor. However, the article does compare AMD and Intel processors in general terms. It mentions that Intel processors tend to have stronger single-core performance, which can be beneficial for certain tasks. However, for AI and deep learning applications, where heavy workload and multi-threaded performance are important, processors with more cores, such as AMD's Threadripper, are often preferred. 

Considering that the Intel i5-4460 is a mid-range processor from the older 4th generation, it may not have the same level of performance or core count as newer processors. While it can still handle general computing tasks reasonably well, it may not be the ideal choice for demanding AI and deep learning workloads. It is always recommended to consult up-to-date benchmarks and consider the specific requirements of your project when selecting a processor.
What are the features mentioned in the article that provide the AI advantage?

The article mentions a few features that can provide advantages for AI applications:

1. PCIe Lanes: The number of PCIe lanes on a CPU is important for AI workstations, as they are primarily assigned to graphics cards (GPUs). AMD's Threadripper and EPYC processors offer higher numbers of PCIe lanes compared to consumer-grade CPUs like Intel's core range, allowing for more bandwidth and the ability to run multiple GPUs simultaneously.

2. Core Count: AMD's Threadripper processors, in particular, are highlighted for their high core count, with up to 64 cores and 128 threads. This can be beneficial for AI and deep learning workloads that require pre-processing datasets and running multiple applications simultaneously.

3. DL Boost: Intel's Xeon processors include a feature called DL Boost, which speeds up deep learning inference operations. It is considered a notable advantage for Intel in terms of DL performance.

4. Deep Learning Software Optimization: Intel has proprietary deep learning software optimization that only runs on Intel CPUs. This can be a factor for those who rely on specific software optimizations for their AI work.

These features are discussed in the article as factors to consider when assessing the AI advantages of different processors.
Elaborate on the deep learning software optimization.

The article briefly mentions that Intel has proprietary deep learning software optimization that only runs on Intel CPUs. Deep learning software optimization refers to specific enhancements and optimizations made to software frameworks and libraries that are commonly used in deep learning applications.

Intel has developed its own set of libraries, tools, and frameworks to optimize the performance of deep learning tasks on their CPUs. These optimizations can include improvements in memory management, computational efficiency, parallelization techniques, and instruction set utilization, among others.

By leveraging these optimizations, Intel CPUs can potentially offer improved performance and efficiency for deep learning tasks compared to competitors. This can be advantageous for users who heavily rely on these specific software optimizations and want to extract the best possible performance from their Intel CPU.

However, it's important to note that the article does not provide specific details about the nature or extent of Intel's deep learning software optimization. It would be advisable to consult official Intel documentation or specific benchmark tests for a more comprehensive understanding of how these optimizations can impact deep learning performance on Intel CPUs.
Can machine learning algorithms simultaneously use CPUs and GPUs or just one or the other?

Machine learning algorithms can utilize both CPUs and GPUs simultaneously, depending on the specific architecture and implementation of the algorithm. 

In many cases, GPUs are preferred for machine learning tasks due to their parallel computing capabilities and ability to handle large-scale matrix operations efficiently. GPUs excel at processing and training neural networks and can significantly accelerate the training and inference stages of deep learning models.

CPUs, on the other hand, are well-suited for handling general computing tasks, including data preprocessing, managing I/O operations, and running non-parallelizable portions of the machine learning pipeline. They are also crucial for orchestrating the overall workflow of a machine learning system.

In practice, machine learning frameworks and libraries provide support for utilizing both CPUs and GPUs together. For example, frameworks like TensorFlow and PyTorch have built-in support for multi-GPU training and allow developers to distribute workloads across different GPU devices. Additionally, frameworks like NVIDIA CUDA and Intel oneAPI provide APIs that enable developers to leverage the combined computing power of CPUs and GPUs seamlessly.

The specific approach to utilizing CPUs and GPUs in combination depends on the algorithm, the specific hardware configuration, and the implementation choices made by the developer. Maximizing performance often involves careful workload partitioning and optimization strategies to leverage the strengths of each type of processing unit.
How does one compare performance between different GPUs, including from different vendors?

Comparing performance between different GPUs, including those from different vendors, involves considering several factors:

1. Benchmark Tests: Benchmark tests specifically designed to evaluate GPU performance, such as those conducted by third-party organizations or technology publications, can provide valuable insights. These tests assess various aspects of GPU performance, including computational power, memory bandwidth, and specific application performance.

2. Specifications: Comparing the specifications of different GPUs can help gauge their relative performance. Key specifications to consider include the number of CUDA cores (for NVIDIA GPUs), core clock speed, memory size, memory bandwidth, and memory type.

3. Application Performance: Understanding how GPUs perform in the specific applications or workloads you are interested in is important. Some applications or frameworks may have compatibility or optimization issues with certain GPUs, while others may work more efficiently on specific architectures.

4. Deep Learning Performance: If deep learning is a focus, comparing GPUs in terms of their performance in neural network training and inference can be crucial. Popular frameworks like TensorFlow and PyTorch often provide benchmarks specifically tailored for deep learning tasks, allowing you to compare relative performance.

5. Reviews and Comparisons: Examining professional reviews, comparisons, and user experiences from reputable sources can provide valuable insights into the real-world performance of different GPUs. These sources may highlight strengths and weaknesses, as well as specific use cases where one GPU may outshine another.

Ultimately, there is no single metric for GPU performance comparison, as it depends on the specific requirements, applications, and workloads you are interested in. Considering a combination of benchmark tests, specifications, application performance, deep learning performance, and expert reviews can help make an informed decision when comparing GPUs from different vendors.
Is there a mathematical formula to compare them?

There is no universal mathematical formula to directly compare the performance of GPUs from different vendors. GPU performance is influenced by a combination of architecture, specifications, software optimizations, and application-specific factors. Thus, a mathematical formula that comprehensively evaluates and quantifies overall performance is not feasible.

However, there are synthetic benchmarks and standardized performance metrics like GFLOPS (floating-point operations per second) and memory bandwidth that provide a rough estimate of GPU performance. These metrics can give you a baseline for comparison, but they do not capture the full range of real-world performance variations across different applications and use cases.

To make a more accurate and meaningful comparison, it is recommended to consult professional reviews, conduct application-specific benchmarks, analyze performance profiles for specific tasks or workloads, and consider factors such as power efficiency and price-to-performance ratio. Additionally, user feedback and experiences can also provide valuable insights into the practical performance of GPUs in specific scenarios.

Ultimately, comparing GPUs requires a holistic approach that takes into account multiple factors and considerations, rather than relying solely on a mathematical formula.
Assume that every GPU runs the same one program, can a mathematical formula be made for that?

If every GPU runs the exact same program, a mathematical formula that directly compares their performance can be derived. In this scenario, the performance comparison would primarily focus on the computational power and efficiency of the GPUs.

One common metric for comparing GPU performance in this context is the theoretical peak performance, often measured in FLOPS (Floating Point Operations Per Second). FLOPS is a measure of the number of floating-point operations a GPU can perform in one second. By considering the GPU's core count, core clock speed, and number of operations per core, you can calculate its theoretical peak performance in FLOPS.

However, it's important to note that theoretical peak performance does not always perfectly translate to real-world performance. Factors such as memory bandwidth limitations, software optimizations, and architectural features specific to each GPU can impact actual performance in practice.

To obtain a more accurate performance comparison, it is advisable to conduct specific benchmark tests on the target program or workload. These benchmarks can measure the execution time, throughput, or other performance metrics that are relevant to the specific
